events.title: Census Bureau Seeks Comments on National Survey of Children's Health
events.summary: The U.S. Census Bureau has issued a notice inviting public comments on the proposed revisions to the National Survey of Children's Health. The revisions include an increased sample size and updated questionnaire content. The survey aims to collect data on the health and well-being of children under 18 in the U.S. Comments are open until February 3, 2026, to ensure consideration before submission to the Office of Management and Budget for approval.
events.why_relevant: This action affects the collection of critical data on children's health, influencing policy and resource allocation.

events.title: Environmental Impact Statements Notice by EPA
events.summary: The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) published a notice of availability for several Environmental Impact Statements (EISs) filed between November 21, 2025, and December 1, 2025. These include final and draft EISs for projects such as the Mojave Exploration Drilling Project and the California High-Speed Rail Project. The notice also includes information on the review and comment periods for these EISs, which are crucial for public and inter-agency feedback on environmental impacts.
events.why_relevant: This notice facilitates public and agency review of significant environmental projects, ensuring transparency and compliance with environmental regulations.

events.title: EPA Approves Texas and Oklahoma Regional Haze Plans
events.summary: The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has approved revisions to the State Implementation Plans (SIPs) for Texas and Oklahoma regarding regional haze. The approval covers Texas's plans for the first and second implementation periods and a five-year progress report, as well as Oklahoma's plan for the first implementation period. These actions are in accordance with the Clean Air Act and the Regional Haze Rule, aiming to improve visibility in national parks and wilderness areas. The final rule is effective January 5, 2026.
events.why_relevant: This approval impacts air quality regulations and environmental protection efforts, affecting visibility and pollution control in national parks and wilderness areas.

events.title: EPA Rescinds Extension of Compliance Deadlines for Coke Oven Facilities
events.summary: The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has rescinded the interim final rule that extended compliance deadlines for National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants (NESHAP) for coke ovens. This action reinstates the original deadlines set in the 2024 rule revisions, requiring compliance with emission standards for coke oven facilities by the previously established dates. The decision follows public comments and a review of compliance challenges, concluding that the extensions were unnecessary.
events.why_relevant: This action affects regulatory compliance timelines for hazardous air pollutants, impacting environmental standards and industry operations.

events.title: FCC Announces Effective Date for Cable Television Rate Amendments
events.summary: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) announced the effective date of January 1, 2026, for amendments to cable television rate regulations under 47 CFR 76.923. These amendments, initially published on July 14, 2025, were delayed pending approval from the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), which was granted on November 26, 2025. The amendments involve modifications to information collection requirements associated with FCC Form 1205.
events.why_relevant: This rule affects the regulation of cable television rates, impacting how rates are set and potentially influencing consumer costs and industry practices.

events.title: FCC Implements New Rate Caps for Inmate Communication Services
events.summary: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) has implemented new interim rate caps for audio and video communication services for incarcerated individuals, as part of the Martha Wright-Reed Act. The rule revises previous rate caps, introduces a new tier for extremely small jails, and includes all safety and security costs in rate calculations. The rule is effective immediately, with compliance required by April 6, 2026.
events.why_relevant: This action addresses the cost of communication services for incarcerated individuals, ensuring fair pricing and compensation, which impacts the economic and regulatory landscape of prison communication services.

events.title: FCC Revises Filing Schedule for Broadcast Applications
events.summary: The Federal Communications Commission's Media Bureau revised the filing schedule for Class A, LPTV, and TV translator major change applications due to a recent government funding lapse. The revised schedule includes temporary freezes and new filing opportunities for these applications, with specific dates for lifting and imposing freezes. This action ensures that applicants have adequate time to prepare and file applications following the disruption.
events.why_relevant: This revision affects the regulatory process for broadcast applications, impacting how and when broadcasters can apply for changes, thus influencing media operations and market dynamics.

events.title: FCC Seeks Comments on Information Collection
events.summary: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) has issued a notice inviting public comments on the information collection requirements under Section 97.213, related to the telecommand of amateur stations. This is part of the FCC's effort to reduce paperwork burdens as mandated by the Paperwork Reduction Act of 1995. The notice seeks input on the necessity, utility, and burden of the information collection, which involves posting station license details for quick resolution of interference issues. Comments are due by February 3, 2026.
events.why_relevant: This action affects regulatory processes and public participation in FCC rulemaking, impacting how amateur radio stations are managed and inspected.

events.title: FDA Announces Availability of Draft and Revised Draft Product-Specific Guidances
events.summary: The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) announced the availability of new and revised draft product-specific guidances for industry. These guidances provide recommendations on bioequivalence study designs to support abbreviated new drug applications (ANDAs). The public is invited to submit comments by February 3, 2026, to ensure consideration before finalization. The guidances are part of FDA's ongoing efforts to provide clarity and support for the development of generic drugs.
events.why_relevant: This action supports the development of generic drugs, impacting drug availability and pricing, which is significant for public health and economic regulation.

events.title: Trump Administration Releases National Security Strategy Supporting Europe's Far Right
events.summary: The Trump administration released a National Security Strategy document that supports Europe's nationalist far-right parties. The document, signed by President Trump, claims Europe faces "civilisational erasure" due to migration and EU integration, and advocates for the US to "cultivate resistance" within Europe. It aligns with the "great replacement" conspiracy theory, suggesting that European countries risk becoming "majority non-European." The strategy emphasizes enabling Europe to take primary responsibility for its defense and opening European markets to US goods and services. The document has drawn criticism from European leaders and US politicians for its controversial stance.
events.why_relevant: This document signifies a shift in US foreign policy towards supporting far-right movements in Europe, potentially affecting international alliances and democratic values.

events.title: U.S. military conducts "double-tap strikes" with lack of transparency
events.summary: Reports indicate that the U.S. military conducted 22 "double-tap strikes" in 2025, resulting in 87 deaths. These operations, targeting drug trafficking, have been criticized for potentially violating international law. The administration has declined to release video footage of the strikes, raising concerns about transparency and accountability in military operations.
events.why_relevant: The lack of transparency in military operations raises ethical and legal concerns about the conduct of U.S. military actions.

events.title: Pentagon conducts lethal boat strike in Pacific
events.summary: On December 4, 2025, the Pentagon announced that the US military executed a lethal strike on a boat in the eastern Pacific Ocean, killing four men. The strike was directed by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and targeted a vessel operated by a Designated Terrorist Organization, suspected of carrying illegal narcotics. This marks the 22nd such strike since September, raising the death toll to at least 87. The legality of these strikes is under scrutiny, with US lawmakers promising to investigate the campaign's legal basis.
events.why_relevant: The event raises significant questions about the legality and ethical implications of military actions against suspected drug smugglers, impacting international law and US military policy.

events.title: US military conducts airstrike on suspected drug smuggling boat in the Caribbean
events.summary: On September 2, 2025, the US military conducted an airstrike on a suspected drug smuggling boat in the Caribbean, resulting in the deaths of nine crew members. Two survivors clung to the wreckage for an hour before being killed in a second attack. The incident was captured on video and shown to US senators, raising concerns about potential war crimes. The Pentagon announced another strike on a similar vessel in the eastern Pacific, killing four men. The legality of these actions is under scrutiny, with debates focusing on whether the strikes comply with international law.
events.why_relevant: The incident raises significant legal and ethical questions about the use of military force and adherence to international law.

events.title: U.S. Military Strike Kills Survivors of Drug Trafficking Boat
events.summary: On September 2, 2025, the U.S. military conducted a strike against a small boat in the Caribbean, killing nine of the eleven individuals aboard. After the initial strike, two survivors were left clinging to the wreckage for 41 minutes before a second strike ordered by Admiral Frank Bradley killed them. The operation has been criticized as unlawful, as the survivors were not engaged in combat and posed no imminent threat. The administration claims the U.S. is in a formal armed conflict with drug cartels, but legal experts dispute this assertion.
events.why_relevant: The incident raises serious concerns about the legality and morality of military actions against individuals not engaged in combat, challenging the principles of due process and the rule of law.

events.title: U.S. Southern Command Confirms Recent Strike Against Drug Boat
events.summary: U.S. Southern Command confirmed a recent military strike against a small boat in the eastern Pacific, stating that the vessel was carrying illicit narcotics and was engaged in narco-trafficking. The strike reportedly killed four individuals identified as "narco-terrorists." This action is part of a broader campaign against drug trafficking operations, which has seen over 20 strikes resulting in numerous fatalities.
events.why_relevant: The confirmation of ongoing military strikes raises questions about the legality and ethical implications of targeting individuals involved in drug trafficking, particularly in the absence of clear combat engagement.
